items.q: In relief printing, which part of the block prints?
items.correct: The raised surface left after cutting away — the cut-away areas stay white.
items.misconception: The carved (cut-away) lines hold the ink and print.
items.random_wrong: The back of the block prints.
items.why: Students assume the lines they cut will print, but relief is the opposite of intaglio: cuts read as white.

items.q: Why do prints come out reversed?
items.correct: The print mirrors the block or plate, so designs — especially text — must be prepared flipped.
items.misconception: Prints come out the same way round as the block.
items.random_wrong: The press rotates the paper 90 degrees.
items.why: Mirroring catches students out, most visibly when lettering prints backwards on the first proof.

items.q: Is each print in an edition just a 'copy' of one original?
items.correct: No — each impression from the matrix is an original; editions are numbered originals.
items.misconception: Yes — only the block is the artwork and prints are reproductions.
items.random_wrong: No — every print in an edition is a unique monotype.
items.why: Students conflate fine-art prints with photocopies; the numbered edition itself is the artwork.

items.q: Lino cutting is which kind of print?
items.why: Correct answer: Relief. “Intaglio” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Inside recessed lines. “On the raised surface” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many main families of printmaking are usually listed?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
